title: AUC: Area Under the Curve
concept: AUC measures the area under the ROC curve, providing a single metric from 0.5 to 1.0 to evaluate a model's general classification ability.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What are the two rates plotted against each other on the ROC curve?

options.text: True Positive Rate on the y-axis and False Positive Rate on the x-axis.
Precision on the y-axis and Recall on the x-axis.
True Negative Rate on the y-axis and False Negative Rate on the x-axis.

exercises.explain: Correct! The ROC curve plots TPR (Recall) against FPR (False Alarm Rate) across all decision thresholds. Let's see how we compress this curve into a single score.

cards.title: Comparing the whole curve
cards.body: Imagine comparing two runners. Instead of checking who is ahead at one specific split second (threshold), you want to know who is faster across the entire race. AUC gives us that single summary score for classifiers.

cards.title: What is AUC?
cards.body: Area Under the Curve (AUC) measures the entire two-dimensional area underneath the ROC curve from (0,0) to (1,1). It ranges from 0.5 (random guessing) to 1.0 (perfect classification).

cards.title: The card sorting test
cards.body: Think of AUC as a model's ability to rank cards. If you give the model one positive card and one negative card, AUC is the probability that the model correctly assigns a higher probability score to the positive card.

cards.title: Spam vs ham ranking
cards.body: If a spam classifier has an AUC of 0.90, there is a 90% chance that a randomly chosen spam email will get a higher spam probability score than a randomly chosen normal email.

cards.title: Does a high AUC mean ready?
cards.body: A high AUC (like 0.95) is great, but it doesn't mean your model is ready for production. You still have to choose a specific decision threshold that fits your business constraints and balances costs.

cards.title: Decoding AUC values
cards.body: How to interpret the AUC score of a model:

left.text:
No better than tossing a coin.
Model cannot distinguish positive from negative.

right.text:
Perfect separation.
All positives ranked above all negatives.

exercises.prompt: What is the AUC score of a model that behaves like a random coin flip?
exercises.explain: An AUC of 0.5 represents a random guesser, corresponding to the diagonal line on the ROC curve.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model with an AUC of 0.95 will always outperform a model with an AUC of 0.90 in every business metric, regardless of the chosen threshold.
exercises.explain: False. While 0.95 has better overall separation, the 0.90 model might perform better at a specific, narrow threshold region that your business actually uses.

exercises.sentence: The maximum possible value for AUC, representing a perfect classifier, is ___.
exercises.explain: An AUC of 1.0 means the ROC curve completely hugs the top-left corner, covering the entire graph area.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is the most accurate description of what AUC measures?

options.text: The probability that a randomly chosen positive instance is ranked higher than a randomly chosen negative instance.
The percentage of correct predictions made at a default threshold of 0.5.
The ratio of true positives to false positives at the optimal threshold.

exercises.explain: AUC is fundamentally a measure of ranking quality: how well the model ranks positive instances higher than negative ones.

exercises.prompt: You are building a medical diagnostic model for a rare condition. The current model has an accuracy of 99% because 99% of people are healthy, but its AUC is 0.52. The business team wants to deploy it. What is your response?

options.text: Reject deployment. The AUC of 0.52 shows the model is barely better than random guessing at separating sick from healthy people.
Deploy it immediately because 99% accuracy is extremely high.
Retrain the model using accuracy as the cost function to boost it to 100%.
Change the target label so that everyone is predicted to be sick.

exercises.explain: Accuracy is highly misleading on imbalanced datasets. An AUC of 0.52 tells the true story: the model has almost no ability to actually distinguish sick patients from healthy ones. 🎉
